skill.name: api-organization
skill.description: Guides teams through a standardized TypeScript API organization pattern with centralized endpoint definitions, typed request and response models, Supabase authentication, and role-aware API clients.
skill.summary: Create consistent, type-safe API layers with Supabase authentication and role-based client patterns.
skill.author: BOM-98

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found the matches are Markdown documentation examples, TypeScript template literals, placeholder URLs, and environment variable names. No executable scripts, prompt injection attempts, obfuscation, credential exfiltration, or malicious intent were found. Publish with a low-risk note because the skill teaches authenticated network client patterns and environment-based configuration.

low_findings.title: Documented Authenticated Network Client Pattern
low_findings.description: The skill documents API clients that send authenticated requests and use environment-based service URLs. This is expected for an API organization guide, but users should ensure generated endpoints only target approved services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network and Authorization header examples are clearly present, but they appear in documentation for legitimate API client setup. No evidence shows unauthorized exfiltration or execution.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The numerous Ruby or shell backtick detections correspond to Markdown inline code, fenced code blocks, and TypeScript template literals. The package contains documentation files, not executable Ruby or shell code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirms the flagged backticks are Markdown or TypeScript examples. I found no script file or instruction that executes shell commands.

low_findings.title: Sensitive File and Windows SAM Findings Are Scanner Misclassification
low_findings.description: The environment file and Windows SAM alerts are caused by documentation text such as required .env.local values and SQL examples. No evidence found that the skill reads environment files, accesses the Windows SAM database, or collects secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation and SQL or environment examples. There is no file access API, registry access, or credential harvesting behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment Variable Use in Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples read public Supabase configuration and API base URL values from process.env. This is normal for Next.js API clients, but generated code should avoid logging or exposing secrets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The process.env usage is directly visible in examples. The risk is low because the referenced Supabase publishable key is public and no secret exfiltration is shown.

dangerous_patterns.title: Bearer Token Construction in Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation shows building an Authorization header from the current Supabase session token. This is a legitimate API auth pattern that requires careful server-side handling.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The token header pattern is explicit and semantically tied to authenticated API calls. It is not malicious, but it is security-sensitive guidance.

content.value_statement: API code often becomes duplicated across endpoints, clients, and authentication paths. This skill provides a clear five-file pattern for typed endpoints, Supabase auth, and role-based access.
actual_capabilities: Explains a five-file API structure for endpoint types, endpoint URLs, clients, admin clients, and protected wrappers.
Shows how to define TypeScript request, response, parameter, and body types for API endpoints.
Describes centralized endpoint URL builders with environment-based API base URLs.
Documents Supabase browser, server, and middleware client setup for authenticated requests.
Provides role-based admin access patterns using a users table and Supabase session checks.
Gives migration guidance for replacing scattered fetch calls with typed wrapper functions.
limitations: It is a documentation skill and does not generate or validate a complete application by itself.
Examples assume Next.js, TypeScript, and Supabase conventions.
It does not include runnable tests or automated schema validation scripts.
Referenced implementation files are named in the guide but are not all included in this package.

use_cases.title: Standardize a New API Domain
use_cases.description: Create endpoint types, URL builders, and protected wrappers before adding feature code.

use_cases.title: Add Supabase Auth to API Calls
use_cases.description: Connect server-side Supabase sessions to authenticated API request headers.

use_cases.title: Review Role-Based API Access
use_cases.description: Check that admin endpoints validate user roles before performing privileged operations.

prompt_templates.title: Create Basic Endpoint Types
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a new resource.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the api-organization skill to define endpoint types for a new resources domain with list, get, create, update, and delete operations.

prompt_templates.title: Add URL Builders
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the api-organization pattern, add endpoint URL builders for my orders API. Include collection, detail, and nested item routes.

prompt_templates.title: Build Protected Wrappers
prompt_templates.scenario: Connect endpoints to typed client functions.
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the api-organization skill to create protected endpoint wrappers that call apiGet, apiPost, apiPut, and apiDelete with TypeScript response types.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Admin Access Flow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review of privileged API access.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my admin API client against the api-organization skill. Check Supabase session handling, role validation, and typed endpoint coverage.

output_examples.output: The response outlines product request types, product response types, endpoint URL builders, and typed wrapper functions for CRUD operations.

output_examples.input: Migrate direct fetch calls to the shared API layer.
output_examples.output: The response identifies scattered calls, maps them to centralized endpoints, and replaces them with authenticated wrapper functions.

output_examples.input: Check whether an admin endpoint is protected correctly.
output_examples.output: The response verifies that the user session is loaded, role data is checked, and privileged requests fail for non-admin users.

best_practices: Define endpoint types before implementing URL builders or wrapper functions.
Keep API base URLs in environment variables and route all calls through centralized clients.
Run admin permission checks before any privileged API operation.
anti_patterns: Do not scatter raw fetch calls across components and server actions.
Do not duplicate endpoint URL strings in multiple files.
Do not rely on client-side role checks for admin-only operations.

faq.question: What framework does this skill assume?
faq.answer: It assumes a TypeScript Next.js application that uses Supabase authentication.

faq.question: Does this skill execute code?
faq.answer: No. It provides Markdown guidance and example code for organizing an API layer.

faq.question: Can it support external APIs?
faq.answer: Yes. The endpoint pattern supports environment-based base URLs for external services.

faq.question: How are admin endpoints protected?
faq.answer: The guide recommends server-side role validation before privileged API requests are sent.

faq.question: Does it include complete client source files?
faq.answer: No. Some referenced client pattern files are mentioned but not included in this package.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
